title: Clinging Back
content:
آ آ آ آ When you see a man come walking down through George Street loose and free,
آ آ آ آ Suit of saddle tweed and soft shirt, and a belt and cabbagetree,
آ آ آ آ With the careless swing and carriage, and the confidence you lack, 
آ آ آ آ There is freedom in Australia! heï؟½s a man thatï؟½s clinging back.
آ آ آ آ Clinginï؟½ back,
آ آ آ آ Holdinï؟½ back,
آ آ آ آ To the old things and the bold things clinging back.

آ آ آ آ When you see a woman riding as I saw one ride to-day
آ آ آ آ Down the street to Milsonï؟½s Ferry on a big, upstanding bay,
آ آ آ آ With her body gently swaying to the horse-shoesï؟½ click-a-clack,
آ آ آ آ You might lift your hat (with caution), sheï؟½s a girl whoï؟½s clinging back.
آ آ آ آ Clinging back,
آ آ آ آ Swinging back.
آ آ آ آ To the old things and the bold things clinging back.

آ آ آ آ When you see a rich man pulling on the harbour in a boat,
آ آ آ آ With the motor launches racing till they scarcely seem to float,
آ آ آ آ And the little skiff is lifting to his muscles tense and slack,
آ آ آ آ You say ï؟½Go itï؟½ to a sane man. Heï؟½s a man thatï؟½s clinging back.
آ آ آ آ Clinging back,
آ آ آ آ Swinging back,
آ آ آ آ To the old things and the bold things clinging back.

آ آ آ آ When you see two lovers strolling, arm-in-arm, or round the waist,
آ آ آ آ And they never seem to loiter, and they never seem to haste,
آ آ آ آ But indifferent to others take the rock or bush-hid track
آ آ آ آ You be sure about their future, theyï؟½re a pair thatï؟½s clinging back.
آ آ آ آ Clinging back,
آ آ آ آ Holding back,
آ آ آ آ To the old things and the bold things clinging back.

آ آ آ آ I, a weary picture writer in a time thatï؟½s cruel plain,
آ آ آ آ Have been clinging all too sadly to what shall not come again,
آ آ آ آ To what shall not come and should not! for the silverï؟½s mostly black,
آ آ آ آ And the gold a dull red copper by the springs where I held back.
آ آ آ آ Clinging back,
آ آ آ آ Holding back,
آ آ آ آ To the old things and the cold things clinging back.

آ آ آ آ But if you should read a writer sending truths home every time,
آ آ آ آ While his every ï؟½pointï؟½ goes ringing like the grandest prose in rhyme,
آ آ آ آ Though he writes the peopleï؟½s grammar, and he spreads the peopleï؟½s ï؟½clack,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He is stronger than the Public! and heï؟½ll jerk the mad world back.
آ آ آ آ Yank it back,
آ آ آ آ Hold it back,
آ آ آ آ For the love of little children hold it back.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
